evidence_quotes.quote: dark practices such as false urgency in a sale.
unfairly skews the terms of the sale and the conditions of ownership in the favour of the publisher.

paragraphs.text: I firmly believe that the problematic practices this initiative aims to tackle are very relevant to our modern day and age. I have personally dealt with especially and in particular misleading marketing from influencers - some willful, some unknowingly - as well as addictive design in digital products, and dark practices such as false urgency in a sale. Where for instance a website would show a sale with a timer counting down from, say, 11 minutes remaining. However refreshing the page a minute later would also reset the timer back to 11 minutes from 10 minutes. The timer on the sale did not exist and was a static element of the page designed to force a sense of urgency. This disincentivizing me as the consumer from reading or investigating whether the product is right for me or whether the product is sold at a fair price, in favour of the merchant benefiting from a likely-bogus 'discount'. However, I think this act is currently leaving out an adjacent and vital other aspect of online practices that have become increasingly common - and with it, increasingly predatory - on the consumer and the consumer's rights. The practice I'm referring to being the attempt to sell software, especially video games, as a digital item but calling it a license that can be revoked at any time with no option for the consumer to retain access to the product in any way shape or form, not even with limited features. For instance, video games are often and primarily sold as a one-time purchase, sometimes with subscription-based access to online service, or "Always-Online" functionality to add features. But publishers attempt to legally define these purchases as a licensing of the video game - and with it, include clauses that give them the ability to revoke access to that purchase at any time for any and sometimes even for no reason, including the end of aforementioned subscription-based or "Always-Online" services. This in effect removing the ability for a consumer to own these digital items. These practices are predatory as they unfairly favour and give considerations to the publisher over the consumers - leaving the latter with no recourse but to lose a product that they have rightfully and legally paid for - in what I would personally characterize as a planned obsolence of the software. I think it is increasingly common in the purveyance of software, including video games, that these primarily one-time purchases are in fact offered only as licenses which can be terminated for, again, any or no reason. This unfairly skews the terms of the sale and the conditions of ownership in the favour of the publisher. It leaves consumers at the whims of these publishers' decision. Should the publisher decide to sell a newer version of their software, they can terminate the former product and thus force consumers to purchase the new product. Currently, there is an ongoing pro-consumer grassroots movement that I support, "Stop Killing Games" - and as a supporter thereof have also signed the EU Citizens Initiative "Stop Destroying Videogames" which mirrors the intention and goals of said movement. And it is as an EU citizen that I would like to ask that the digital fairness act also considers adding protections for consumers. Protections to defend them against these practices that can for any or no reason terminate access to a legal copy of a video game or indeed other software. It is a hindrance to both preservation of video games as an art form, but principally a major infringement on what I believe is a basic right of the consumer: that I own the (digital) item I have purchased. Legislative protection of the consumer, including curtailing attempts to circumvent this basic right through deceptive language and unfair business practices, would be a greatly appreciated and natural extension of this initiative's stated goals, with the issues it already aims to tackle. Thank you for your time and effort in protecting consumers from unfair practices.
